title: Supercollimating photonic crystal scintillators
authors: Seou Choi, Sachin Vaidya, Charles Roques-Carmes, Marin Soljačić
abstract: Scintillators convert X-ray energy into visible or near-visible photons, enabling applications in high-energy particle detection and X-ray imaging. Increasing scintillator thickness improves X-ray absorption but degrades spatial resolution due to diffraction-induced lateral spreading of emitted light, resulting in a fundamental trade-off between detection efficiency and image resolution. Here, we propose a class of three-dimensional photonic crystal scintillators that overcomes this limitation through supercollimation, in which light propagates with suppressed diffraction. We develop a multiscale modeling framework that integrates nanophotonic band-structure simulations with Monte Carlo particle transport to quantitatively evaluate the performance of such scintillators. Our results show that supercollimating photonic crystal scintillators can enhance spatial resolution by up to an order of magnitude relative to conventional bulk scintillators of equal thickness. This improvement leads to a substantial increase in detector quantum efficiency (DQE), particularly at high spatial frequencies, enabling fine features to be preserved at reduced X-ray dose. We further demonstrate that comparable image quality can be achieved with approximately an order-of-magnitude lower X-ray dose. By directly engineering light transport within the bulk of the scintillator, this work establishes a nanophotonic route to simultaneously improving resolution and dose efficiency in X-ray imaging.
summary: This paper proposes using 3D photonic crystals to engineer the direction of light emission in scintillators through supercollimation. By suppressing the lateral spreading of light, these structures allow for thicker, more efficient X-ray detectors without sacrificing image resolution. This breakthrough could enable high-quality medical imaging at significantly lower radiation doses.
main_problem: The fundamental trade-off in X-ray scintillators where increasing thickness improves X-ray absorption but degrades spatial resolution due to diffraction-induced lateral light spreading.
main_result: 3D supercollimating photonic crystal scintillators can improve spatial resolution by up to an order of magnitude and reduce the required X-ray dose by approximately 10-fold while maintaining image quality.
method: A multiscale modeling framework integrating nanophotonic band-structure simulations (MPB) with Monte Carlo particle transport (Geant4) to evaluate light propagation and X-ray imaging performance.
model_or_system: A 3D single gyroid photonic crystal made of scintillating materials such as Lu2O3(Eu) or ZnSe(Te), designed with flat isofrequency contours to achieve supercollimation of emitted light.
key_observables: Spatial resolution (via Point Spread Function and Modulation Transfer Function), Detector Quantum Efficiency (DQE), Feature Similarity Index (FSIM), and X-ray dose.
important_parameters: Isofrequency contour (IFC) flatness, supercollimation bandwidth, X-ray energy (e.g., 30-33.5 keV), and refractive index contrast.
main_assumptions: Light emitted outside the supercollimation bandwidth is assumed to be emitted isotropically; the detector is assumed to be an ideal silicon detector with 100% quantum efficiency.
figures_summary: Fig 1 illustrates the concept of supercollimation vs. bulk spreading; Fig 2 shows band structures, IFCs, and MIT logo imaging; Fig 3 compares emission spectra and DQE for different materials; Fig 4 demonstrates kidney vasculature imaging and dose reduction via FSIM scores.
paper_structure: Introduction of the resolution-efficiency trade-off, description of the 3D photonic crystal design and supercollimation mechanism, presentation of the multiscale simulation framework, results comparing performance to bulk scintillators, and demonstration of dose reduction in medical imaging simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. High spatial resolution X-ray imaging with supercolli- mating photonic crystal scintillators. (a and b) Scintillation in (a) a bulk scintillator and (b) a supercollimating scintillator. (a) High en- ergy particles such as X-rays create a dipole that emits visible light. Scintillating light propagates with lateral spreading, due to a circular isofrequency contour (IFC) (panel I), reducing the spatial resolution of X-ray images (panel II). (b) Tailoring the IFC to have a flat sur- face toward the back plane of the scintillator, scintillating light can be collimated (panel I), enhancing the spatial resolution of X-ray im- ages (panel II).

FIG. 2. Supercollimation in photonic crystal scintillators. (a) Band structure of the three-dimensional single gyroid photonic crystal scintillator calculated using MIT Photonic Bands (MPB)26. Insets show the photonic crystal structure and the first Brillouin zone. Isofrequency contours (IFCs) of the first two bands are shown in the right panel. (b) X-ray sources shaped through a small pinhole and the MIT logo are used (left panel). X-ray images generated by the photonic crystal scintillator (middle panel) and the bulk scintillator (right panel) of equal thickness are shown. The thickness of the scintillator is 300 µm. Refractive index, scintillation yield and X-ray attenuation are modeled...

FIG. 3. Detector Quantum Efficiency (DQE) enhancement in supercollimating photonic crystal scintillators. (a and d) Emission spec- trum and the supercollimation bandwidth for (a) ZnSe(Te) and (d) Lu2O3(Eu). (b and e) Simulated X-ray images for different thicknesses of the scintillator. The upper (lower) row of each sub-figure shows X-ray images of the MIT logo generated by supercollimating photonic crystal scintillators (uniform scintillators). The thickness of the scintillator in the three panels is 1.5µ, 3.0µ and 4.5µ, where µ is the attenuation length of each scintillator (µ ∼130 µm for ZnSe(Te) and µ ∼60 µm for Lu2O3(Eu) at X-ray energy of 30 keV). (c and f) Point spread function (PSF),...

FIG. 4. Enhancing medical X-ray imaging performance using supercollimating photonic crystal scintillators. (a) Direct X-ray detection of a human kidney vasculature (reference). (b) X-ray images of the human kidney vasculature obtained under different X-ray doses with supercollimating photonic crystal scintillators (upper panel) and uniform scintillators (lower panel). The exact X-ray dose of each image is marked in (c). (c) Feature similarity index (FSIM) score dependence as a function of X-ray dose. Supercollimating photonic crystal scintillators can achieve similar FSIM score while using ∼×10 less X-ray dose. The thickness of the scintillators considered here is 270 µm. For the X-ray...
